region_of_origin: China (Hunan / Sichuan regional origin; served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sautéed pork with chili peppers; closest parent 'pork dish' (FOODON_00002764) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for stir-fried pork with chili peppers; 'Pig meat products (cooked)' is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific prepared pork dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sautéed pork with chili peppers is approximately 250 g, comprising sliced pork belly and chili peppers served as a main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with cooking-loss and seasoning adjustments for stir-fried preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base macros derived from USDA SR Legacy pork belly (raw) values adjusted for stir-frying cooking loss (~20% moisture loss) and addition of chili peppers, garlic, soy sauce, and oil. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish. Values cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork and published nutrient composition of similar Chinese stir-fried pork dishes. This is an estimated composition; an exact match for 辣椒炒肉 was not found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA as a composite dish entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and vegetable stir-fry dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate cooked pork and chili pepper composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sautéed pork with chili peppers. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. The dish contains approximately 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from chili peppers and minor seasoning; protein and fat content dominate the macronutrient profile, suppressing glycemic response. GL per 250 g serving = (15 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce seasoning (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a standard 250 g serving contributes approximately 1300 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sautéed pork with chili peppers is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the dish itself has a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.2 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~5.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat content from pork belly and high sodium from soy sauce seasoning; choosing leaner pork cuts and reduced-sodium soy sauce can improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sautéed Pork with Chili Peppers (辣椒炒肉, là jiāo chǎo ròu) is a classic Chinese stir-fried dish originating from Hunan province and widely popular across China and in Chinese restaurants throughout Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying thinly sliced pork belly — prized for its layered fat-to-meat ratio — in a hot wok with fresh chili peppers, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sometimes fermented black bean paste. The cooking technique is rapid high-heat wok frying (sautéing), which produces a slightly charred, aromatic exterior on the pork while retaining juiciness. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, spicy, and umami-rich, with the chili peppers providing both heat and a slight sweetness. The texture contrasts tender pork slices with the slight crunch of partially cooked chili peppers. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.5 g per 100 g) and fat (~16.2 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.2 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI ~15). The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content. It is typically served as a main dish alongside steamed white rice in a Chinese meal setting.
common_user_queries: What is sautéed pork with chili peppers?
How many calories in stir-fried pork with chili?

Can I eat chili pork with Type 2 Diabetes?

confidence_reason: No exact composite dish entry for 辣椒炒肉 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data with cooking-loss adjustments and seasoning estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database classification framework for low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food type and is not reliably estimated for stir-fried pork dishes. All other -1 fields in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values where defensible data exists. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100 g) reflects typical soy sauce seasoning used in restaurant preparation and may vary significantly by recipe. The dish is served at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles in Singapore at SGD 18.00. field survey data was used only as a source of food identity and restaurant context, not as a nutrient source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy and adapted for stir-fried preparation; an exact approved-source match for this composite dish was not available. GL calculation: GI (15) × available carbohydrate per serving (8.0 g for 250 g serving) / 100 = 1.2.
